introHtml: System design interviews are open-ended and assess architecture thinking. You need to reason about scale, trade off consistency vs availability, choose storage engines, design for failure, and explain your decisions clearly. Practice the common archetypes: URL shortener, news feed, chat application, rate limiter.

topics.bodyHtml:
Latency Numbers Every Engineer Should Know

OperationLatency
L1 cache reference~0.5 ns
L2 cache reference~7 ns
Main memory (RAM) access~100 ns
SSD random read~150 µs
HDD seek~10 ms
Network same datacenter~500 µs
Network cross-region~30-100 ms
Network cross-continent~150 ms

Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

AspectVertical (Scale Up)Horizontal (Scale Out)
MethodMore RAM/CPU on same machineMore machines
LimitHardware ceilingNear-limitless
CostExpensive at high endCommodity hardware
ComplexitySimpleDistributed systems complexity
DowntimeOften requiredRolling deploys possible

Idempotency: same request produces same result regardless of how many times sent. Use idempotency keys on payment/mutation APIs: client sends unique key, server deduplicates.

Load Balancers

// L4 (Transport): routes by IP + TCP port — fast, no content awareness
// L7 (Application): routes by HTTP headers, URL, cookies — smarter, SSL termination

// Algorithms:
// Round-robin: distribute evenly (default)
// Weighted: more traffic to powerful servers
// Least connections: route to server with fewest active connections
// IP hash: same client always hits same server (session affinity)
// Consistent hashing: minimal reshuffling when nodes added/removed

Circuit Breaker Pattern

// CLOSED: requests pass through, monitoring error rate
// OPEN: all requests fail fast after threshold exceeded
// HALF-OPEN: probe request; success closes, failure reopens

class CircuitBreaker {
  constructor(fn, { threshold = 5, timeout = 60000 } = {}) {
    this.fn = fn; this.state = "CLOSED"; this.failures = 0;
    this.threshold = threshold; this.timeout = timeout;
  }
  async call(...args) {
    if (this.state === "OPEN") throw new Error("Circuit OPEN");
    try {
      const result = await this.fn(...args);
      this.failures = 0; this.state = "CLOSED";
      return result;
    } catch (err) {
      this.failures++;
      if (this.failures >= this.threshold) {
        this.state = "OPEN";
        setTimeout(() => { this.state = "HALF-OPEN"; }, this.timeout);
      }
      throw err;
    }
  }
}

// Read replicas: primary handles writes, replicas handle reads
// Replication lag: replicas may be slightly behind (eventual consistency)

// Consistent hashing: minimize data movement when adding shards
// Hash ring: each server assigned position on ring
// Data: hash key, find nearest clockwise server
// Virtual nodes: each physical server has multiple ring positions

// PgBouncer: PostgreSQL connection pooler
// Transaction mode: most efficient — connection returned after each txn
// Limits active DB connections while supporting many app connections

// Database per service (microservices):
// No cross-service DB joins — use API calls or events to combine data

Consistency: Every read returns the most recent write or an error.
Availability: Every request receives a response (not necessarily most recent).
Partition Tolerance: System continues despite network partitions.

In distributed systems you must tolerate partitions. Real choice: CP vs AP.

SystemTypeTrade-off
PostgreSQL (single node)CANot distributed
HBase, ZooKeeper, etcdCPMay reject reads during partition
Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDBAPMay return stale data

PACELC: Even without partition, there is a Latency vs Consistency trade-off.

// CQRS: Command Query Responsibility Segregation
// Commands: modify state, return nothing (or ID)
// Queries: return data, no side effects
// Separate read/write models optimized differently

// Event Sourcing: store events, derive state by replaying them
const events = [
  { type: "Deposited", amount: 100 },
  { type: "Withdrawn", amount: 30 }
];
const balance = events.reduce((acc, e) => {
  if (e.type === "Deposited") return acc + e.amount;
  if (e.type === "Withdrawn") return acc - e.amount;
  return acc;
}, 0); // 70

// Outbox Pattern: guarantee message delivery
// 1. BEGIN TRANSACTION
// 2. INSERT business data
// 3. INSERT INTO outbox (event_type, payload, status='pending')
// 4. COMMIT
// 5. Background worker: poll outbox, publish to queue, mark sent

// Saga Pattern (distributed transactions):
// Choreography: each service reacts to events from others
// Orchestration: central coordinator issues commands to each service

URL Shortener

// Short code: Base62-encode auto-increment ID
// 2009215674938 -> "zn9edcu" (7 chars)
// Schema: id (BIGINT), short_code (VARCHAR UNIQUE), original_url (TEXT), created_at
// Cache: Redis GET shortcode -> original_url (high read:write ratio)
// Redirect: 301 (permanent, cached) vs 302 (temporary, always hits server)

Rate Limiter

// Token bucket: refill at fixed rate, burst allowed
// Leaky bucket: requests processed at fixed rate, smooth output
// Sliding window (Redis sorted set):
const now = Date.now();
await redis.zremrangebyscore(key, 0, now - windowMs);
const count = await redis.zcard(key);
if (count >= limit) throw new RateLimitError();
await redis.zadd(key, now, `${now}-${uuid()}`);
await redis.pexpire(key, windowMs);

Chat Application

// WebSocket: persistent bidirectional connection
// Message broker (Kafka): fan-out messages to recipients
// Presence: Redis hash { userId -> socketId, lastSeen }
// Heartbeat: client pings every 30s
// Message delivery:
// At-most-once: fire-and-forget (may lose messages)
// At-least-once: retry until ack (may duplicate — use idempotency keys)
// Exactly-once: hardest — dedup at consumer with unique message IDs

News Feed

// Fan-out on write: when Alice posts, push to all follower feeds in Redis
//   Pro: fast reads; Con: expensive writes for celebrities (millions of followers)
// Fan-out on read: user requests feed, server merges posts from followees
//   Pro: cheap writes; Con: slow reads
// Hybrid: fan-out on write for regular users, fan-out on read for celebrities

# Dockerfile best practices (multi-stage)
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS production
WORKDIR /app
RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
USER app
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]

# Kubernetes essentials:
# Pod: smallest unit (1+ containers sharing network/storage)
# Deployment: declarative desired state + rolling updates
# Service: stable DNS + load balancing to pod set
# Ingress: HTTP routing rules (path/host to service)
# ConfigMap: non-sensitive config as key-value
# Secret: base64 sensitive data (encrypt at rest with KMS)
# HPA: autoscale on CPU/memory/custom metric

# Deployment strategies:
# Rolling: gradual replacement, brief mixed versions
# Blue/Green: two envs, instant switch, double resources
# Canary: small % to new version, monitor errors, then promote

# Observability (three pillars):
# Metrics: Prometheus scrape + Grafana dashboard
# Logs: ELK stack or Loki + Grafana
# Traces: OpenTelemetry -> Jaeger / Tempo (distributed tracing)

items.question: What should you clarify before drawing boxes in a system design interview?
items.answerHtml: Clarify product scope, traffic assumptions, read-write ratio, latency goals, consistency expectations, retention, compliance, and critical user flows. Strong candidates spend the first few minutes narrowing the problem so the design answers the real constraints instead of a generic internet-scale fantasy.
items.flashcardBackHtml: Clarify requirements, constraints, and scale before proposing components.

items.question: How would you explain CAP theorem without oversimplifying it?
items.answerHtml: During a network partition, a distributed system must choose whether to reject some requests to preserve strong consistency or continue serving potentially stale data for availability. The key nuance is that partition tolerance is not optional once the system is distributed, so the real tradeoff is usually consistency versus availability under failure.
items.flashcardBackHtml: In distributed systems you assume partitions can happen, so the tradeoff is usually consistency versus availability during that failure.

items.question: When is fan-out on write better than fan-out on read for a feed system?
items.answerHtml: Fan-out on write is better when reads are extremely frequent and follower counts are manageable because it makes reads fast. Fan-out on read is better for celebrity-style skew or when write amplification would be too expensive. Many practical systems use a hybrid model.

items.question: How do you decide whether a bottleneck should be solved with caching, sharding, or asynchronous work?
items.answerHtml: Look at the workload shape. Caching helps repeated reads of hot data. Sharding helps when a dataset or write volume no longer fits a single node. Asynchronous work helps when user-facing latency should not wait for slow or non-critical side effects. The right answer depends on whether the pressure is read throughput, write throughput, storage size, or tail latency.

items.question: Why is idempotency a recurring theme in large-scale design questions?
items.answerHtml: Retries happen everywhere: load balancers, clients, workers, and queues all retry on timeouts or failures. Without idempotency, retries turn transient failures into duplicate writes or double charges. Good designs assume retries are normal and make repeated processing safe.

items.question: How would you compare read replicas with sharding in one sentence and then one paragraph?
items.answerHtml: In one sentence: replicas scale reads of the same dataset, while sharding splits the dataset itself across nodes to scale storage and write throughput. In a longer answer, emphasize that replicas improve read capacity but introduce replication lag, whereas sharding increases operational complexity around routing, rebalancing, and cross-shard queries.

items.question: Design a URL shortener. What are the first five things you would say out loud?
items.answerHtml: I would clarify expected traffic and read-write ratio, decide whether custom aliases are allowed, choose an ID generation strategy, add a redirect cache such as Redis, and discuss analytics, abuse prevention, and expiration as secondary features after the core redirect path works.

items.question: A chat system must show presence, ordered delivery in a room, and offline catch-up. Which subsystems would you call out immediately?
items.answerHtml: I would call out persistent connections such as WebSockets, a message broker or append-only log, durable storage for history, Redis or similar for presence and ephemeral state, and client or server logic for ordering and acknowledgements. Then I would talk about delivery guarantees and reconnect behavior.

items.question: How would you respond if the interviewer says: now make the system multi-region and highly available?
items.answerHtml: I would revisit the consistency model first because multi-region changes correctness as much as infrastructure. Then I would discuss region-local load balancing, replication strategy, failover behavior, conflict handling, data sovereignty if relevant, and what latency-sensitive traffic should stay local versus go cross-region.

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: Always clarify requirements, estimate scale, and identify bottlenecks before proposing an architecture
CAP theorem: distributed systems can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance
Horizontal scaling + stateless services + load balancer is the standard baseline for high-traffic systems
CDNs serve static assets from edge nodes — they reduce latency and offload the origin significantly
Database read replicas and read-through caches handle read-heavy workloads without scaling writes
Idempotency keys prevent duplicate operations when clients retry failed requests
Event-driven architecture decouples services but requires careful handling of ordering and at-least-once delivery
